data.title: Precigen: UltraCAR-T Development Brings Another Side Of Pipeline Advancement
data.description:
The last time I wrote about Precigen (NASDAQ:PGEN) it was with respect to a Seeking Alpha article entitled "Precigen: RRP Targeting With AdenoVerse Technology Platform". In this article, I wrote about the opportunity for this company to file a Biologics License Application [BLA] filing of PRGN-2012 for the treatment of patients with recurrent respiratory papillomatosis [RRP]. I'm happy to state that not only was such a regulatory filing submitted to the FDA, but was accepted by it with a Priority Review PDUFA date of August 27th of 2025. The future of this program bodes well, because for the time being the FDA has not set an advisory panel to review this drug. While this is a good program for investors to focus on in the near-term, I believe that great potential lies with another type of technology in the company's pipeline.

This would be with respect to the development of UltraCAR-T to target and kill cancers in a far more effective manner. The gist of this is that the technology, if deployed with clinical success, would disrupt the CAR-T landscape. I will be going over several reasons why below, but the promise of this is another aspect that investors should keep an eye on going forward. This program can be divided into two different advancements. There is UltraCAR-T PRGN-3006 which already had enrollment completed for a phase 1b study targeting patients with acute myeloid leukemia [AML]. Matter of fact, the company is set to speak to the FDA on next steps for this program advancement.

The other UltraCAR-T in the pipeline is PRGN-3008 which is set to target CD19 tumors in oncology and has versatility to also be used to target patients with autoimmune disorders. Preclinical data presented established proof-of-concept of this candidate achieving substantially superior efficacy compared to conventional CD19 CAR-T. The only thing to consider though, for the time being, is that the company is only set to advance its Ultra-CART platform with a partner. The reason why is because in August of 2024 it established a pipeline reprioritization where it would only put its sole focus on the commercialization efforts of PRGN-2012 for the treatment of patients with RRP. However, at the same time, it did highlight that it is in discussions with potential partners to get these UltraCAR-T candidates developed.

PRGN Submission Being Accepted With No Advisory Panel Bodes Well For Potential Approval

As I stated above, Precigen was able to submit its Biologics License Application [BLA] of PRGN-2012 for the treatment of patients with recurrent respiratory papillomatosis [RRP]. This was done on the basis with Accelerated Approval only with a pivotal phase 1/2 study that was completed, which used this AdenoVerse gene therapy to treat these patients. Before going over the data from this pivotal study, plus any catalysts to be released for this specific program, it is first important to go over what this disorder is and what the possible market opportunity for the company could be. Recurrent Respiratory Papillomatosis [RRP] is a type of disorder characterized as the growth of benign tumors that grow in a person's respiratory tract. Even more pronounced, they can sometimes also grow in the voice box [larynx] of a patient as well. What is responsible for causing this disease would be that of human papillomavirus [HPV]. More specifically, HPV6 and HPV11, which are responsible for the formation of such benign tumors. There are two ways whereby this disorder can come about, which are either through sexual contact or passed along from mother to child during birth. There are a host of symptoms that patients with RRP might experience and they are as follows:

  • Not being able to breathe effectively
  • Chronic cough being present
  • Change in voice
  • Growth of benign tumors obstructing air passage way
  • Potential Growth of invasive cancers

In terms of market opportunity for this indication, it can be very large. This would be based on what the company notes in its presentation on slide 9, where it indicates how many patients it could go after in the United States. Plus, the number of RRP patients it could treat with PRGN-2012 in other parts of the world. Such numbers are as follows:

  • 27,000 adult RRP patients in the United States
  • Greater than 125,000 patients ex-U.S.

The current standard of care [SOC] for treating these RRP patients, believe it or not, is surgery. The attempt to remove these benign growths is what is currently available for them. If this is the case, then why would they instead need a treatment like PRGN-2012? It is because surgery doesn't help at all, because there is a high rate of recurrence afterwards. Not only that, but some patients might even need to have surgery every few weeks. The lucky ones might be able to go for over a year or more, but they will eventually need surgery again. With no FDA approved drug in place for these RRP patients, this company has a chance to be the first to give these patients one with PRGN-2012.

The reason why the company was able to submit a rolling Biologics License Application [BLA] submission of PRGN-2012 to treat these RRP patients was on the basis of a phase 1/2 pivotal study, as I noted directly above. This particular trial recruited up to a total of 35 patients. Patients that were recruited into this trial received 4 injections of 5X10^11 particle units [PU] before receiving debulking surgery. That is, this drug was given as an adjuvant before the patients had some parts of their benign tumor removed through surgery. In essence, "debulking" is removing as much as the tumor as possible in place without completely eradicating it. The primary endpoint being looked at for this study was the number of patients who achieved a complete response after the debulking surgery was done. For a patient to be considered to have achieved a complete response, they would need to have no surgical intervention for at least 12 months following treatment. The data was highly ideal, because in the end it was noted that approximately 18 out of 35 patients [51%] of them achieved such a Complete Response [CR]. Not only that, but a huge majority of them [86%] of them had reduced surgical interventions in the year after treatment with PRGN-2012. This, compared to the prior year where such patients had to have a lot more surgical interventions to remove benign tumor growths. This efficacy was achieved with no treatment related adverse events [TRAEs] being above Grade 2, which is another bonus of this drug.

The acceptance of the BLA filing was highly ideal, not just because it was accepted by the FDA, but it was done so with Priority Review. Having said that, the U.S. agency set a PDUFA date of August 27, 2025. What's even better, which I believe increases the odds of approval, would be that no advisory panel has been set to go over review of the drug for the treatment of these patients with RRP. While this doesn't guarantee U.S. marketing approval of PRGN-2012, it does at least remove a hurdle that would have otherwise been in the way. I must reiterate that these patients need a new treatment option, because they have to go through surgery on a constant basis. Not only does it increase the risk of damage to the larynx, but could also result in other morbidities.

UltraCAR-T Program In Place Provides Another Shot On Goal

It remains to be seen whether or not the company receives FDA approval of PRGN-2012 for the treatment of patients with RRP, but it does have another program in its pipeline that is quite promising to the say the least. This would be in terms of it developing UltraCAR-Ts. The promising aspect of this technology, should it be successful, would be for it to completely change the CAR-T landscape. Consider that there is an ability to enhance each problem that remains with CAR-Ts currently available or still in clinical development. Such advantages using its UltraCAR-T platform technology could be:

  • Non-viral multigenic delivery system - ability to establish large multi-gene cargo load with high efficiency; This allows for 1 day manufacturing compared to autologous and allogenic CAR-Ts that require 4-weeks to manufacture
  • In-vivo expansion and persistence - a problem with current CAR-Ts is that they have no persistence whatsoever. To that end, this company has been able to incorporate a membrane bound IL-15 that is responsible for promoting T-cell expansion and persistence

The thing about the UltraCAR-T in place is that it has a "kill switch" deployed. This is put in place to eliminate the CAR-Ts if necessary to improve upon safety of it. The end result of this technology is to eliminate T-cell exhaustion and hopefully increase response rates. All of this, which as I described above, to provide an autologous CAR-T type treatment that is capable of being manufactured in one day. The company had noted that enrollment for a phase 1b study was completed with PRGN-3006 targeting patients with AML. The company hopes to engage with the FDA on potential next steps for this program. Another UltraCAR-T is known as PRGN-3008 and it is being developed to target CD19 directed solid tumors and/or autoimmune disorders. As far as a catalyst goes for this program, it would be whether or not a collaboration or partnership agreement can be made. The company stated in its most recent Q3 of 2024 financial earnings report that it is solely focused on PRGN-2012 advancement and that it is in talks for further financing either through a strategic partnership or another such similar transaction.

Financials

According to the 10-Q SEC Filing, Precigen had cash and cash equivalents of $24.7 million and short-term investments of $3.9 million as of September 30th of 2024. The company was able to close a public offering in August of 2024, which ended up resulting in net proceeds of approximately $30.9 million. The thing is that during the same month the company enacted this offering, it also announced a strategic prioritization of its pipeline. It reduced 20% of its workforce and it chose to only focus on the advancement of PRGN-2012 for the treatment of patients with RRP alone. However, in this 10-Q SEC Filing "on page 27" it notes that it is focused on forming a strategic partnership for its UltraCAR-T program. It is currently in discussions, but there is no guarantee of it being able to complete such a partnership. It also enacted a securities purchase agreement for the sale of 8.00% of its Series A Convertible Perpetual Preferred Stock in a private placement. This helped the company to raise approximately $79 million before deducting expenses. This isn't going to help the company fund itself for a long time either. Why is that? That's because this company burns roughly $36.4 million of cash per quarter.

Risks To Business

There are several risks that investors should be aware of before investing in Precigen. The first risk to consider would be in terms of the BLA filing of PRGN-2012 for the treatment of patients with recurrent respiratory papillomatosis. The risk here is that despite a Priority Review date being set by the FDA, there is no assurance that the company is going to receive U.S. marketing approval of this drug for the treatment of these patients.

The second risk to consider would be in terms of the advancement of PRGN-3006 for the treatment of patients with Acute Myeloid Leukemia [AML]. With the company having completed enrollment in the phase 1b study using this UltraCAR-T to target these patients, it is set to meet with the FDA to discuss next potential steps. The risk here is that there is no assurance that the FDA is going to like the data it has seen thus far. Nor, that it will allow Precigen to move forward in using this clinical candidate to treat these AML patients in a subsequent study.

The third and final risk to consider would be in terms of the development of UltraCAR-T PRGN-3008 as a next generation CAR-T. The goal of the company is to be able to develop this candidate to target both CD19 expressing solid tumors and autoimmune disorders. Thus, the first risk here is that there is no guarantee that this type of CAR-T is going to work in either of these two general indication categories. Another risk is that there is no assurance that the company will be able to advance either of these programs in the clinic. As I stated beforehand, the company won't advance its UltraCAR-Ts unless it can find a partner who is willing to fund it. Lastly, the whole point of the UltraCAR-T technology is to have several of the advantages that I listed above. Being able to generate persistence, safety/kill switch, IL-15 membrane binding, increased manufacturing time frame and others. Thus, the risk here is that despite all of these enhancements, there is no assurance the company will ultimately yield superior CAR-T candidates over other biotechs in the same space. Nor, that such modifications are going to allow the company to report upon an improved clinical outcome either.

Conclusion

Precigen has done well to advance the use of PRGN-2012 for the treatment of patients with recurrent respiratory papillomatosis or RRP. That's because not only did it get the FDA to accept its rolling BLA submission of this gene therapy candidate, but it was able to do so with Priority Review with a PDUFA date of August 27, 2025. The hope is that it is going to be able to find a partner that will be willing to fund its UltraCAR-T program though. The reason why is because of what I listed above, in that this technology is very promising with all the modifications that it has been able to do with CAR-T development. I think that investors should be particularly intrigued with the fact that it might be able to deploy autologous CAR-Ts such as PRGN-3008, which can be manufactured in 1 day. Again, other CAR-Ts have a manufacturing time frame of about 4-weeks. With the BLA submission of PRGN-2012 being accepted by the FDA with a Priority Review date of August 27th of 2025, plus the unique nature of its UltraCAR-T program it has in place, I believe that investors could benefit from any potential gains made here.

1 Effective 10/31/25, Kathryn Langridge no longer serves as portfolio manager of the fund.

2 It is not possible to invest in an index. Index definitions can be found within the "What you should know before Investing" section on the last page of this fact sheet.

3“Net (what you pay)” represents the effect of a contractual fee waiver and/or expense reimbursement and is subject to change.

4 Listed holdings reflect the largest portions of the fund’s total and may change at any time. They are not recommendations to buy or sell any security. Data is expressed as a percentage of net assets and excludes cash and cash equivalents.

The performance data shown represents past performance, which does not guarantee future results. Returns for periods shorter than one year are cumulative, and results for other share classes will vary. Shares will fluctuate in value and, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original cost. Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance cited. Performance figures assume that all distributions are reinvested. For the most recent month-end performance, visit j hinvestments.com.

Market review and outlook

Emerging-market stocks’ fourth-quarter decline reflected concerns about slowing global growth and a possible shift toward less accommodative monetary policy by the U.S. Federal Reserve. Latin America lagged considerably, weighing on the broader asset class. Brazil was hurt by worries about its growing fiscal deficit, and Mexico came under pressure from rising protectionism in the United States. In Asia, Korea was a notable laggard due to the emergence of political turmoil.

India and most smaller Asian markets suffered losses, as well. On the positive side, Taiwan—a continued beneficiary of strength in the global technology sector—performed well, as did select oil-producing nations in Africa and the Middle East.

We believe events in the United States, particularly tariffs, will be a key factor for the emerging markets in 2025. China and Mexico are most at risk from such actions, while India’s economy appears relatively insulated. China’s fiscal stimulus has disappointed some investors so far, but by keeping some powder dry, it can fine-tune its response based on U.S. trade policy. Aside from trade, we believe China’s main policy ambition will be to shore up the housing market and restore consumer confidence. Although Indian stocks declined in late 2024, we believe the country remains on track for long-term growth. Still, we’re cognizant of extended valuations in some areas of the market. In Brazil, we believe the companies the fund holds are led by excellent management teams and are positioned to deliver attractive long-term returns despite the challenges the market has faced over the past year. Looking more broadly, we think a key theme will be the sustainability of the artificial intelligence boom and the impact on technology companies in South Korea and Taiwan.

Contributors and detractors

Stock selection in India made the largest contribution to relative performance, led by PB Fintech Ltd., MakeMyTrip Ltd. (MMYT), and Coforge Ltd. (CFRG), among others.

Selection in Taiwan also contributed thanks to holdings in eMemory Technology, Inc., MediaTek, Inc. (OTCPK:MDTTF), and ASE Technology Holding Co., Ltd. (ASX) Positioning in Argentina further helped results, led by a position in the IT services company Globant SA. (GLOB)

On the other hand, selection in Brazil detracted. At a time in which investors displayed concern about the direction of interest rates, the bank stocks Itau Unibanco Holding SA (ITUB) and Banco BTG Pactual SA were the principal detractors. Selection in China detracted, primarily as a result of positions in Centre Testing International Group Co., Ltd., Kweichow Moutai Co., Ltd., and Kanzhun Ltd. (BZ) All three stocks participated in the third-quarter rally but subsequently gave back some of those gains as investors became more pessimistic about the Chinese government’s efforts to revive economic activity.

Portfolio changes

We remain focused on identifying high-quality companies that can deliver long- term growth. We continue to add to or reduce existing holdings based on our fair value estimates, while initiating positions in new stocks that fit our criteria as the opportunities arise. The fund’s outperformance in the volatile fourth quarter gives us confidence that the portfolio is well positioned to weather potential trade wars and other shocks in the year ahead.

data.title: OTC Markets Group: The Moat-Rich, Undervalued Trading System Operator Under Our Noses
data.description:
Summary

OTC Markets Group (OTCQX:OTCM) operates various alternative trading systems, with a steadfast moat, 36% discounted PE, with few non-systematic risks involved. OTCM is a business you should own forever - earnings power/free cash flow generation will continue to compound as the years go on, and therefore stockholder returns will follow. This is explained in the coming sections. The opportunity exists as the market has overlooked what’s staring them in the face, again. This could be for any number of reasons, but none of them are particularly sensical.

The Business

Business Description

The OTC Markets Group operates alternative trading systems for (typically) smaller enterprises. Essentially, they’re a stock exchange, but they’re not a ‘national exchange’ like the NYSE or Nasdaq. They, to comprise the model, have three subsidiaries:

OTC Link provides three ATSes: OTC Links, OTC Link ECN, and OTC Link NQB. Each of these provides trading services to broker-dealer subscribers.

Market Data Licensing provides data on listed businesses through third-party data distributors and the businesses’ website.

Corporate services operate the OTCQX Best Market and OTCQB Venture Market, who offer a variety of services including OTC Disclosure and news service, OTCIQ, quote displays, and virtual investor conferences.

Note: each exchange’s specifics bear no relevance on the thesis.

The Moat

OTCM’s moat is both wide and deep – competitors may try to cross it, but they’re likely to exhaust themselves along the way. This has afforded them a lasting competitive advantage:

A legally enforced monopoly within their niche: While there are alternatives, that disregards listing requirements. OTC markets are unusual in the sense that there’s no listing requirements, unlike e.g. the NYSE’s $100mn mkt cap and 3yr earnings min. $10mn requirement. This has allowed the OTC markets to become comprised of smaller businesses in need of equity financing, which is their niche. Identical exchanges may form, but the US regulation is such that it takes years for any competitor to gain their footing. Obviously, OTC aren’t going to lower their drawbridge – they have the reputation of being the only reliable US option, so why give that up? Long story short, it’s practically impossible for competitors to invade their niche. It helps that the regulators aren’t incentivised to approve another identical market (it makes their job harder, and there’s no real industry progression to make it worthwhile), and potential competitors aren’t incentivised to try and compete anyway. I don’t suspect you’d find competing with OTCM any better of an idea than competing with NYSE if you had the chance. Reputation, especially of a business with a sound operating history, makes businesses largely set in their position.

Customer Dependency: OTCM has (fortunately) avoided the risk typical to most businesses, the risk that customers can be lost at a large scale for effectively any reason. OTCM generates revenue from listing businesses/subscription fees for being continually listed, which means OTCs data is being used. Their subscription model is incredibly simple, but the specifics aren’t worth knowing – it doesn’t bear on the value present. For OTCM to make money, a business must be looking for equity financing, which could be for any variety of reasons (which aren’t of relevance here). Managers often, by default, forget how to manage the business without the availability of cash, and while they may do very well with these funds, they’re left with little choice once the time comes. This leaves OTCM continually generating subscription fees with expandable pricing power and minimal revenue downside, providing businesses don’t delist due to fraud or ‘special situations’ (which isn’t even nearly frequent enough to be an issue). For more, see the ‘Financials’ section

Network Effects: As with any market operator, OTCM work with broker-dealers. OTCM’s reputation, and monopoly of their niche, means brokers have to operate with OTCM to do what they’re paid for. They could always go to other markets e.g. NYSE, but they’d be dealing in different securities there, so it depends on what they can sell better than the next guy. Due to OTC securities, as a class, being less liquid, manually processed, and pricing set bid-ask spreads, Broker-Dealers typically receive higher commissions, which incentivises them to deal here instead of the overcrowded (not to mention overpriced) markets elsewhere. History has made it clear they care more about commission than customers (as is the nature of their profession). This isn’t a dig at OTC securities/brokers (most of my investments are OTC), but you get a lot of ignorance there – hence the opportunity.

Scalability: as per their subscription model, they already have the infrastructure necessary to list/provide data services to businesses - they don’t have to set up another office, build a factory, or any other usual capex. The marginal effort required is largely a matter of accumulating the right data and documents. This is significantly less than the marginal reward, which is mostly continual payments until the businesses delist (for reasons previously mentioned). This, effectively, is operating leverage. We’re now offered an apt explanation for their historical, and likely ongoing, margin expansion.

The inverse nature of the investment: As an investor in OTCM, you’re essentially relying on private businesses being poorly managed, and so being reliant on equity financing when the going gets tough. This means that, unlike 99.9% of other businesses (arbitrarily speaking), OTCM is likely to benefit more in a depressed economy, so when businesses are more likely to need cash (there will be a slight lag). The retort I’d expect is ‘but shouldn’t that even out, as they downturn when things look/actually are more prosperous?’. However, their subscription fees will continue to provide a hedge as 84% of 2024Q1-3 revenue. In short, they win when things look bleak, and barely lose out when things look brighter, which provides them (and so, by extension, investors) with an enticingly asymmetric corporate risk-return profile.

Financials

Note: any averages, unless otherwise stated, go back to 2008 – this is as far back as their public data goes, and (not so coincidentally) when ‘Pink Sheets’ became ‘OTC Markets’. Their IPO was in 2010, which was the right move – public businesses changing names draws a suspicious crowd.

Income Statement

OTCM generates revenues based on a subscription service (84%) and transactions (16%), which affords OTCM largely recurring revenues. Their subscription dons as a typical model – investors/businesses require all the services so they have the information necessary to abide to, and make best use of, the listings. The only upkeep required is updating their large information database and making sure their websites don’t crash (which has proved more than manageable), but reinvestments are probable. The downside of any subscription model is the trade-off between growth and stability. OTCMs income statement is not likely to see any sudden growth, but I see no issues with a slow and steady compounder. As OTCM is relatively undervalued (see ‘Comps Valuation’), the growth is a bonus to our returns anyway. I suppose, by this point, you’re probably wondering what the numbers are, so without further ado:

OTCM’s revenue, as established, is positively correlated with how many businesses list/stay listed on OTC markets, albeit in a slightly skewed, but cyclical, fashion: New listings often follow a depressive general environment of some sort (2010 and 2021 made this very apparent, with listings being 41% and 42% above the average respectively). New Listings has increased 27%* on average p.a. (see form 211 filings, not to be confused with a filing in and of itself e.g. a 10K). So, while there’s been significant volatility in listings, subscriptions are the vast majority of revenues (I’ll avoid repeating myself). It is unusual that, while growth varied, there hasn’t been a down year since 2008 (as far back as data is available, but I’d take it with a pinch of salt). The average revenue increase is 11.3% (a 10.86% CAGR), which isn’t ideal, but given the circumstances, is acceptable all things considered.

*including dual securities

OTCM’s Q1-3 2024 operating margin was 21%, while their average is 28% (rounded). Considering the recent corporate activity e.g. Acquisitions of Blue Sky and Edgar, it makes sense – they have been allocating capital towards reinvesting into the business. Exchange businesses tend to follow this pattern, but these reinvestments are a longer-term bet on cash generation, so it doesn’t worry me. Given at least a year, operating margins could return to the normalised levels (base case), or possibly peak if this speculation repeats history (our best proxy).

Net income followed a similar pattern - OTCM has demonstrated the ability to generate satisfactory profit on their operation. Their average net margin is 20%, in which the trend has been satisfactory, but by no means smooth. The average net income growth is 23% p.a. (an 18% CAGR, which matches the state of their revenues). There have been down years, but has never, bar one case, dropped below the net margin level it had two years ago (take this with a pinch of salt or two). Any business that experiences completely linear growth comes across as Enron-esque (something I could not dig up any conviction for). OTCM is good, but not too good to be true. If you wait long enough, something will go wrong eventually.

Balance Sheet

Liabilities

OTCMs balance sheet is incredibly simple – there’s very few items to consider (as is typical of exchanges).

Around half of the liabilities are deferred revenues (non-current). While this is a high proportion, the nature of the subscription model in general offers an apt explanation.

The management have done excellently in ensuring OTCM are debt-free since 2010 (something they don’t share with most of their peers of similar activity, who tend to attempt expansions). Usually, I’d have more to say on debt, but it’s hard when there isn’t any.

The rest of their liabilities are accounted for by standard expenses/income tax reserves.

There are no off-balance sheet arrangements or preferred shares (there is only Class A.)

Assets

Being software-based, their tangible asset requirements are incredibly low.

OTCM, as you may have guessed, have a stable balance sheet. They’re somewhat capital intensive, which puts some pressure on their cash pile, but they have enough to cover total liabilities. Their cash pile has grown at 13% avg p.a., which I expect to continue, especially as markets continue to become more speculative. See the Cash Flow Statement section for what they could do with this cash.

It shouldn’t come as a surprise that their non-current assets are mostly intangible, the most important being property and equipment: lease right-of-use assets, deferred tax, and IP. The proportions of these have stayed constant enough over the years to be contributed to the nature of their business model, but they are a permanent necessity for generating a revenue.

Generating and Allocating Cash

OTCM have paid a 4% dividend yield, with a dividend coverage of 1.55. The dividend yield has varied in tandem with operating results, but nothing out of tune e.g. the 2024Q1-3 dividend yield was 1% (a 75% decrease from the year prior), which closely matches the decrease in dividend payments. This shows shareholders aren’t too erratic (at least for now), but taking the general overvaluation of the markets into account, this could turn at any time. OTCM have paid a special dividend the last five years, which I’ve factored into the yield. The management doesn’t see a better use of their cash, which I don’t entirely agree with (then again, as insiders, they’re bound to know things I don’t.)

I would prefer, mostly for the tax-free nature, for repurchases to be prioritised. While repurchases have been executed when the management believes the stock is below their guess at fair value (which isn’t always, necessarily, accurate), the exercising of stock options means that overall Class A (the only class) common stock has increased 15% total since 2008. However, even upon the exercise of all the outstanding options, it would only cost OTCM 1% of 2024 annualised NI (a 2% dilution)*. As this is essentially insider buying (providing the options aren’t sold early, which would indicate inside pessimism), it doesn’t worry me. I’ll happily trade off some dilution for more aligned interests.

*Note: I used the WA price of total options outstanding, exercisable in any year, net of the current price.

OTCM have made two note-worthy acquisitions in the last five years. First, Blue Sky Data Corp, which was acquired for the sake of licensing their data (it is licensed as a ‘compliance data products’), which fits nicely into their ecosystem. Their second acquisition was EDGAR Online, which many of you may recognise from your time spent in the SEC system. This was done in partnership with the SEC and Nasdaq, which succeeded in making their customers more accessible to PA investors. These acquisitions were fairly uninterrupted, and while it’s more of a bonus to the thesis, having good relations is always a good hedge against competitive forces (especially when regulation is involved).

OTCM make their acquisitions more to prevent operating issues, and to protect their moat, than to make some giant leap forward. This is the sort of conservatism I admire in a management – it’s much easier to keep up with value creation than to make up for your losses. I’d note that a giant leap forward, so to speak, isn’t really possible anyway. This raises the question of whether it is admirable if they have no choice.

OTCMs average ROC (EBIT/ (NWC+NFA)) is 116%, which has increased at a 6.48% CAGR each year. While the CAGR is not great, it can’t be thought of as a growth rate of any sort, and going from good to better is less of an issue – the margin of safety is more than satisfactory. Regardless, I find it of interest as it shows the businesses ability to effectively (or in most cases, not so effectively) allocate their capital to the benefit of their long-term operating results. At first glance, OTCM doesn’t seem like something that would fit this measure, but they are heavily reliant on their property and equipment (mostly just furniture and tech, which has been D&Ad over time.)

Their Earnings Yield (EBIT/EV) is somewhat of a disadvantage, which averages 9%. I say ‘somewhat’ as the business is wonderful, and is often the case with long-term compounders. This is mostly the product of a return to normalised EBIT, after a craze of businesses in need of equity financing after 2020/2021. The current EY is 5%, which is explained in the line above. I don’t weight this metric highly for a business of this sort, but in case it was of interest.

What particularly caught my eye was their return on equity. Their ROE (automatically unlevered) averaged 67%, which I consider exceedingly high. My initial thought was something like ‘it’s too high’, but it has increased very steadily since 2008, which shows the management competence in relation to using our cash for us. Again, with this year being 57%, we’re below par, but bad years are inevitable. If this wasn’t the case, we would not have this opportunity.

Comp Valuation

OTCM, going off earnings, is undervalued relative to its peers. Considering earnings is all that drives long-run stock prices (provided the absence of long periods of mania, which is always possible), this is of interest. While some variation may stand out at you, that’s much more to do with their country’s economic situation than the merits of their own business – they have been assigned illogical prices.

Note: figures are rounded in the respective currencies

OTCMs PE is 23. If they reach the industry average (which they should eventually, if market history upholds), we’re presented with a 57% upside*. I prefer using an industry average - it eliminates having to think about if each business considered is over/under valued, which can be done at a glance, but to no accurate extent. The fewer considerations the better - there’s less chances for mistakes that way.

*Note: I cannot give a timeline on when this value will be realised – people are too unpredictable, but the opportunity is not diminished.

Management

Judging by the moat (qualitative and quantitative) OTCM has dug, it’s apparent their management is broadly effective (the management composition hasn’t really changed as far back as we look). They’re critical thinkers and honest people – they address their mistakes first, how they came about (more importantly), and what’s being done about them.

The insider ownership offers great insight into how aligned management-stockholder interests are, which I regard as a great preserver of shareholder wealth. Insider Ownership is as follows:

Name

Author: Oliver Mitchell, Source: Annual report 2023

Management compensation is reasonable, and it’s not too much to misalign interests. They each get paid for their/their departments achievements. It’s not worth knowing the specifics, but the general principle is of relevance.

Further aligning interests is the ‘2019 Equity Incentive Plan’, which was implemented in 2022 (I know). The arrangement is fairly simple, and does what it’s supposed to – OTCM is obligated to deliver fixed monetary amounts equivalent to ‘Performance Units’ when certain bottom-line targets are met. Each performance unit represents the right to receive one class A stock (50% vested in cash, 50% claimed). This gives employees the same desired outcome as shareholders, which is the only acceptable situation. If this ever changes, I will very quickly issue an update, and recommend further actions (most likely to sell the stock, or at least never buy if you’ve dodged the bullet).

Risks – our own ‘caveat emptor’

IPOs slowing: If IPOs were too slow, OTCM would forego listing fee revenue, which is consistently of at least a mild importance. I say ‘mild’ because, per 2024Q3 data, listing fees are 16% of revenues, which makes a material difference to the cash flow generation of the business. I do not consider this a large risk – private businesses, as a class, will always be poorly managed, otherwise they wouldn’t need to list in larger flocks each economic cycle. History has made it clear this risk will stay mild (see new listing figures, income statement section.), but that doesn’t mean that couldn’t change.

Concentrated insider ownership: The overall 35% insider ownership is favorable, but R. Coulson owning 28% (partially justified by his being president and CEO) puts us overly exposed to his interests. While all evidence points to our interests being aligned with his, there’s always the possibility it could change e.g. he could turn to aggressive accounting policies to boost stock price while he cashes in on options (I am not saying he will do this). There is also the possibility that his interests remain aligned with ours, but human error gets its grip. Either way, the stock would likely be subject to overreactions pointing downwards, and our annualised return would be lowered. However, we could always make up for it by purchasing more of the depressed stock, and lengthen our holding period accordingly.

Regulation: The largest problem with regulation is that, while timely announcements and news coverage dresses it up as an orderly affair, it’s really one big mess. If you look at the history of exchanges, and the businesses listed on them, you’d quickly realise that there isn’t always the most justifiable reason for these sudden changes in required operation changes. My favourite example of this is how it can be cheaper, as a small firm, due to your low op costs, to violate and pay the fines than make the required investments into whatever it may be. If changes are slower paced, OTCM’s management has enough time to figure out their first port of call for the sake of protecting themselves, but the same cannot be said for those on the more sudden side of things. This may lead to OTCM having to restrict some activities/be more in depth in others, which would change the long-term composition of their revenues. If this occurs, the hedge they get from subscriptions being most of their revenue may dwindle, and their moat eroded. Due to its unpredictable nature, I can’t provide specifics, but the general principle is that their top/bottom line will be challenged. This would harm their CF generating ability, which may slow down OTCM being recognised for its merits, and erode value along the way.

Note: I excluded risks that aren’t idiosyncratic to the business - they don’t bear on OTCM’s merits. There’s little practical sense in devoting them any thought beyond deciding to ignore them – they are too complex, and therefor unpredictable (at least for me.)

Approach for Purchase

My approach is to buy as long as it's relatively cheap on a PE basis compared to its peers, and there’s no dissuading material changes in the meantime. If it ever reached the average industry PE, I’d revaluate, but that wouldn’t necessarily dissuade me of the business’s merits (I’d be more likely to focus more on intrinsic value after that). If it drops, I’d continue to buy the stock up until I feel my other positions are too diluted. I have no other price targets or deadlines in mind – I prefer to keep things simple, and I’m happy to hold this for as long as I have a portfolio (providing no adverse material changes). It’s very obviously undervalued, and there’s enough margin to forego the need for a specific target price.

Other Points of interest

Avg 3-month Daily Volume: $227k

C.10% of the business is held by Funds (Isthmus, Argent CM LLC, and Legato CM LLC), along with some mutual funds (per Yahoo!). The Mutual Funds will have it as part of an index of some sort, in which T Row Price Small-Cap Value Fund stands out.

Analyst coverage is limited, which is mainly due to a $626mn mkt cap, and OTC listed. It’s just not enticing enough for the ‘analysts’ out there – OTCM wouldn’t write them the check.

10-k release is March (dates vary), which may help clear the picture for the rest of the market.

Catalyst

The business, primarily the bottom line, returning to normalised levels as reinvestments from acquisitions start to contribute to operating leverage.

Value can be its own catalyst. The market will recognise the mistake they have made, more likely when the value creation/earnings outpace prices, but until then, our opportunity will expand with time.

Editor's Note: This article was submitted as part of Seeking Alpha's Best Small/Micro Cap Idea investment competition, which runs through February 28. With cash prizes, this competition -- open to all analysts -- is one you don't want to miss. If you are interested in becoming an analyst and taking part in the competition, click here to find out more and submit your article today!

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Inflation Falls Sharply In France As Consumer Spending Contracts
data.description:
By Charlotte de Montpellier

Consumer price inflation stood at just 0.8% in France in February, down sharply from 1.7% in January. This is the first time since February 2021 that French inflation has been below 1%.

The fall in inflation is mainly due to the 15% fall in regulated electricity prices, which pushed energy inflation down by 5.7% in February 2025 compared with February 2024. Other price categories are more stable. Food inflation picked up slightly by +0.3% year-on-year, compared with 0.1% in February 2024; the price of manufactured goods remained stable, and services inflation continued to slow to 2.1% YoY, compared with 2.5% in January and 3.2% in December.

Generally speaking, inflationary pressures are currently extremely weak in France and inflation is unlikely to rebound significantly in the coming months, which should reassure the European Central Bank. The weakness of inflation, and in particular the marked slowdown in growth in the price of services, points to a weak French economy where demand is very sluggish. This was confirmed once again this morning by the January consumer data, which showed a 0.5% fall in household spending on goods over the month in volume terms, following the 0.7% rise over the month seen in December. Despite the sales period, purchases of clothing and durable goods fell sharply over the month (-1.7% and -3.9% respectively).

The first quarter has therefore gotten off to a poor start. After a contraction of 0.1% in the fourth quarter, the risk of a further contraction in the first quarter cannot be ruled out. Nevertheless, the slight improvement in business sentiment in February could point to a slightly better month than January. If this were to continue in March, GDP could stabilise in the first quarter and avoid a further contraction.

Overall, the economic situation in France remains very poor. While inflation is clearly no longer a problem for the French economy, the weakness in activity is a very clear one. At this stage, there is little sign of a real recovery for the rest of the year. The marked deterioration in the employment climate – which has reached its lowest point since March 2015 as a result of the deterioration in business hiring prospects – is a very clear sign that economic momentum will remain weak in the coming quarters. We are expecting GDP to grow by 0.6% in 2025, after 1.1% in 2024.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

Original Post

data.title: Bargain Alert: 2 Big Dividends, Up To 10% Yield
data.description:
Co-authored with Hidden Opportunities.

There are many things the news cycle loves to keep us preoccupied with. After all, they get paid to keep us on the edge of our seats. The Federal Reserve's next moves remain a hot topic; inflation may have moderated but still hasn’t disappeared, and it appears to show signs of rebounding. Everyone likes to make projections of the potential impact of President Trump's tariffs, and recession probabilities continue to fluctuate, with JPMorgan (JPM) recently raising its forecast to 35%. Historically, economists rarely peg the likelihood of a recession above 90% or below 10%, which underscores the inherent difficulty in precise forecasting.

Predicting what will happen in the markets is easy; predicting when it will happen is nearly impossible. Market sentiment is shifting rapidly – some investors are piling into stocks, believing that rate cuts are imminent and that a booming economy fueled by tax cuts and incentives for American businesses is on the horizon. Meanwhile, others remain cautious, concerned about lingering economic risks, rising unemployment, weakening consumer confidence, and persistent inflation.

This is precisely when disciplined, income-focused investors can thrive. After all, when times are tough, cash flow is king! Hence, instead of trying to time the market, at High Dividend Opportunities, we prioritize building a resilient income stream from sectors that are vital to the national economy. Let’s dive into our top picks:

Pick #1: THQ – Yield 10.4%

2025 will be an interesting year for healthcare costs. Experts estimate commercial healthcare spending to grow to its highest level in 13 years. PwC’s Health Research Institute is projecting an 8% YOY medical cost trend, driven by inflationary pressure, prescription drug spending, and behavioral health utilization.

Note: The medical cost trend is defined as the projected percentage increase in the cost to treat patients from one year to the next, assuming benefits remain the same.

Healthcare is a massive industry in the United States, with FY 2023 expenses exceeding $4.9 trillion, reflecting a YOY growth rate of 7.5%, or about $14,570 per person, according to a CMS report. Over the past decades, medical care prices have generally grown faster than overall consumer prices, making it the biggest cause for concern among retirees. Source.

When we discussed abrdn Healthcare Investors (HQH) earlier this month, we discussed the growing adoption of new and expensive treatments like GLP-1, above-inflation price hikes planned for popular prescription drugs, increasing consumer eligibility for expensive and advanced treatments like cell and gene therapies, and an overall rise in chronic diseases among the American population. These are structural tailwinds for inflation-beating rise in healthcare costs for the years ahead.

abrdn Healthcare Opportunities Fund (THQ) is a CEF (Closed-End Fund) that invests in 111 holdings in pharmaceuticals, healthcare equipment, biotech, and managed healthcare services, among other essential subsectors of the healthcare industry. The CEF’s top ten holdings represent some of the largest medical insurance, biopharma, and medical device companies in the United States, representing 45% of the invested assets. Source.

THQ has been delivering steady $0.1125 monthly distributions to shareholders since 2014, which was raised to $0.18/share after the acquisition by Aberdeen last year, calculating to a current 10.4% annualized yield. Through ten years of its operating tenure, the CEF has outperformed its passive indices, mainly from large and predictable monthly distributions.

In its annual report dated Sept 30, 2024, THQ reported $150 million in unrealized gains on its investments. The CEF distributed $78 million to shareholders during FY 2024 while experiencing a $111.3 million change in net assets. Notably, since its inception in 2014, THQ has distributed $14.97/share while growing its NAV by 9%, making it a well-managed and high-performing investment in the healthcare sector.

Healthcare demand in the U.S. will grow for the foreseeable future, and the sector is likely to experience significant inflation in the coming years. It doesn’t matter who is heading the Federal agency that governs health services, and their stance on vaccines. With THQ, you can gain exposure to top-performing companies and collect steady income from the steady and necessary demand for healthcare services.

Pick #2: NNN – Yield 5.4%

NNN REIT Inc. (NNN) reported its Q4 and full-year earnings on February 11. The company ended FY 2024 with 3,568 freestanding single-tenant properties with an occupancy of 98.5%. During the fourth quarter, the REIT invested $217 million in acquiring 31 new properties at an initial cap rate of 7.6% with a weighted average lease duration of 20 years. During the quarter, the company also disposed of $150 million worth of assets at a 7.3% cap rate. Acquiring at a high cap rate while making dispositions at a lower cap rate can be a very profitable move, as explained below.

So when you acquire more real estate assets at high cap rates (good deal for the buyer), you are basically making good deals in relation to how much rental income you can collect. Turning around and selling other assets at low cap rates (good deals for a seller) makes you a net buyer of positive cash flows from your investments.

For FY 2024, NNN delivered 1.8% YOY core funds from operation (“FFO”) growth while making over $550 million in acquisitions. Core FFO and AFFO (adjusted FFO) were $3.32 per share and $3.35 per share, respectively, placing its 2024 dividend payments ($2.29/share) at a 68% AFFO payout ratio. The REIT reported $196 million of free cash flow after the payment of all expenses and dividends!

NNN boasts 35 years of consecutive annual dividend increases, and the REIT maintains an investment-grade balance sheet with a sector-leading 12.1-year weighted-average debt maturity, effectively shielding the company from near-term changes in the interest rate policy. NNN’s next debt maturity is in November 2025, and the REIT funded 61% of its acquisitions with FCF and proceeds from dispositions.

Resilient Recovery

One of the elements of NNN’s operational results from Q3 that caused a lot of FUD for investors were its troubled tenants, Frisch and Badcock Home Furnitures. During Q4, NNN made significant progress with these assets.

NNN had 32 properties leased to Badcock, generating $5.2 million in annual base rent, representing 0.6% of the ABR at the start of Q4. The company completed its going-out-of-business sales, and rejected the leases on all 32 properties. By December 31, NNN was able to release five of those properties at ~70% rent recovery, again, with no TI (Tenant Improvements) for the vacancy release. TI typically refers to any modifications or improvements made to the property to make it more appealing or suitable for a new tenant. Additionally, NNN sold six of the Badcock properties, generating net proceeds of $21.8 million (reflecting a 5.1% cap rate if we consider the originally agreed-upon rent). With these transactions, NNN minimized downtime for 35% of the distressed tenant’s properties.

NNN began Q4 owning 64 properties leased to Frisch's Big Boy Restaurants, representing 1.5% of the ABR ($12.6 million). This tenant did not file for bankruptcy, forcing the REIT to go through a time-intensive process of repossessing its stores through evictions. NNN has initiated that eviction process for all 64 stores and, as of year-end, had possession of 33 stores. 28 of those stores have been released to another restaurant operator, minimizing downtime for ~44% of the leased assets. Overall, NNN is making the best of a bad situation, which is inevitable in the business world. The REIT’s portfolio diversification and overall demand for its properties is demonstrated in the limited impact on earnings, maintenance of overall occupancy rates, and minimization of occupancy through quick releasing activity.

2025 Guidance

NNN has issued guidance for FY 2025, and the REIT projects core FFO at a range of $3.33 to $3.38 per share and AFFO guidance with a range of $3.39 to $3.44 per share. Assuming a 3% dividend raise in Q3, the REIT’s annual payout for FY 2025 will be placed comfortably at a 69% payout ratio at the midpoint of the AFFO guidance range. NNN projects $500-$600 million in acquisitions and $80 to $120 million of dispositions during FY 2025.

Overall, NNN reported a fantastic quarter, demonstrating tangible recovery from its tenant challenges, and the REIT’s strong balance sheet and its deeply diversified portfolio are well-positioned to fuel growing dividends for shareholders.

Conclusion

In uncertain markets, consistent and predictable cash flow is king. While THQ provides exposure to the ever-growing healthcare sector, delivering strong distributions backed by a diverse portfolio of healthcare companies, NNN offers a reliable stream of rental income from high-quality retail tenants, reinforcing its track record of consistent dividend growth. By focusing on essential sectors and prioritizing steady cash flow over market speculation, we position ourselves for long-term financial stability – regardless of where the broader economy moves next.

At High Dividend Opportunities, we maintain a highly diversified portfolio of income-producing securities to enable a stress-free retirement. Some of our holdings do well in a strong economy, while others thrive in a weak economy. It is impossible to predict what we will face in the near term. Hence, we protect ourselves from the unknown with diversification. With more than 45 securities in our model portfolio, we tap into income streams across all sectors of the economy. We call this approach the Income Method – a mission built to deliver lifelong income and long-term financial peace of mind.

data.title: Comfort Systems USA: A Wonderful Business With Sound Growth Prospects
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Comfort Systems USA (NYSE:FIX) is a wonderful mid-cap enterprise with a wide economic moat, great earnings power and what seems to be some impressive growth prospects.

Their massive set of operating units, extensive provision of products and services across the lifecycle of HVAC, plumbing and electrical systems and absolute focus on being a productive operation suggests that the firm should be capable of producing outsized returns on invested capital and equity over the next ten years.

While the valuation certainly is not cheap on a relative analysis, I believe that the present market value of future free cash flows is excessively pessimistic with a potential 42% undervaluation currently existing in shares.

Due to my admiration and appreciation for the business, growth and valuation, I rate FIX stock a Buy.

Business Overview & Economic Moat

Comfort Systems is an industrial corporation that produces a wide range of mechanical, electrical and plumbing (MEP) systems for buildings.

Products produced by the firm include HVAC (heating, ventilation and air conditioning) systems, fire suppressant solutions and the installation of complex building-wide electrical systems.

Comfort Systems splits these products into two categories: mechanical (which includes HVAC and plumbing products) and electrical services. The mechanical segment products almost 80% of all revenues for the firm with the final 20% coming from electrical services.

I think the mechanical segment is the more moat of the two due to the more complex system requirements these products hold compared to electrical structures and thanks to the greater maintenance needs associated with plumbing and HVAC units.

Production of systems along with the expertise required to install them into buildings requires a large and knowledgeable workforce, significant manufacturing capacity and extensive relationships within the industry. Comfort Systems appears to have all three with the firm currently having over 178 locations, 18,200 skilled employees and 136 separate city-centric operating units in the U.S.

Furthermore, Comfort Systems offers a holistic service to customers whereby the firm can design, construct, install, expand and renovated MEP systems across a wide range of industrial, commercial and institutional buildings.

While other smaller competitors such as Limbach (LMB), AAON (AAON) and IES (IESC) offer similar services to customers, Comfort Systems is by far the largest (over three times the size of near rivals IES) and offers the most comprehensive set of solutions to customers.

I have come to this conclusion through analysing the diverse portfolios of products and services each company offers and was impressed with Comfort Systems. The firm has over 45 separate operating units with each individual company leveraging powerful local economic of scale and repetitional images to attract customers.

Furthermore, each unit benefits collectively from the shared access to resources, capital and knowledge which further bolsters their capabilities relative to smaller-scale rivals.

These business units also cater to customers across a range of cyclical end-markets such as technology, manufacturing, education, healthcare, office buildings, retail, government and multi-family residential.

I was also very glad to see that maintenance, repair and replacement services constitute a massive portion of Comfort System’s operations and is one of the business operations which produces the most moat for the firm, in my opinion.

While the company’s new-build business is critical to establishing client relationships and provides 60% of revenues, 40% comes from their maintenance operations which I suspect are also more profitable as Comfort Systems USA would enjoy significant pricing power due to the high switching costs consumers would face.

Collectively, I see Comfort Systems enjoying a wide economic moat thanks to their extensive product range, significant operational scale generating cost advantages and due to the switching costs associated with customers transitioning away from their solutions.

Fiscal Analysis

Compared to rivals, Comfort Systems has the highest five-year mean ROA, ROE and ROICs at about 11%, 27% and 23% respectively. Their nearest rival IES only manages returns of about 10%, 25% and 20% respectively while smaller rivals Limbach are down about 10pp and 8pp when compared to Comfort Systems and IES.

These metrics support my hypothesis that Comfort Systems has a wide economic moat that allows the firm to enjoy pricing power and long-term relationships with customers which in turn enables the company to generate additional earnings per client through maintenance.

Comfort Systems also has a WACC of about 12% which, compared to their ROIC of 23%, suggests that the firm is effectively generating real value above the rate of inflation (about 3%) which is critical for operational sustainability.

Gross, operating and net margins of 19%, 6.5% and 6% also support my thesis that Comfort Systems’ larger scale affords a cost advantage to the firm when negotiating with suppliers and producers. IES appears to have similar margins while the smaller Limbach is once again down about 3pp relative to its larger rivals.

This overview of the firm’s operating economics suggests that at its core, Comfort Systems is a profitable and healthy business. The 10-K for 2024 published on February 20 was also mostly positive with the firm producing superb revenue and operating income growth.

Revenue increased for the full-year by 35% YoY to $7 billion thanks to what management deemed was a high-demand environment as well as due to the beneficial impact of new acquisitions.

The significant demand witnessed in the technology industry came due to the construction of new chip manufacturing and data warehouses which require extensive and complex HVAC and electrical systems.

The organic growth contributed about 23% of the total 35% YoY figure with the remaining 12% coming from the acquisition of Summit, J&S, DECCO and Eldeco companies. These acquisitions all fit perfectly into their core MEP operations and I therefore view them as strategically viable.

COGS as a percentage of revenues actually decline 2pp YoY to 79% thanks to the very strong revenue generation in 2024 as well as due continued emphasis on cost savings by management. I suspect the integration of new acquisitions into the Comfort Systems structure may take between 1-3 years so additional profitability from each acquisition would logically increase slightly as the years progress.

Operating expenses also remain approximately constant at between 10-11% of revenues which left operating income at $749 million implying an operating margin of 11%. While such operational growth is impressive, it must also be recalled that the new business done in 2024 will help bolster future growth even more as additional maintenance sales increase.

I was also pleased to note that their order backlog increased 16% YoY with most of their growth coming from the more moaty mechanical segment. Robust demand is indicative of a competitive product and service arrangement while the opportunity for future maintenance revenues only further sweetens the current situation at Comfort Systems.

Seeking Alpha’s Quant supports my broad and 2024 specific analysis by providing the firm an “A-“ profitability grade at present time which suggests that the firm is operating with sound enterprise economics.

As of February 14, Comfort Systems also has a robust balance sheet and what I deem to be an excellent balance sheet and capital allocation strategy.

Total current assets of $2.8 billion are handily ahead of current liabilities which amount to just $2.6 billion. This leaves the firm with an excellent current ratio of roughly 1x with the firm’s producing an additional $836 million in levered FCF in 2024. Such massive cash generation abilities combined with great short-term liquidity provides economic validity to their acquisitionary capital allocation strategy.

Comfort Systems also has just $68 million and change in long-term debts with a majority of these debentures being on attractive fixed-rates and maturing after 2025. This seems to me to be strategic use of low-cost capital which I always appreciate from businesses.

Last year saw Comfort Systems spend about $110 million on share repurchases and dividends cumulatively which constitutes about 15% of EBIT. The company also spent $94 million on acquisitions in the fiscal year which I believe were astute purchases of companies that directly contribute to their core operational competencies.

This strict focus on core MEP companies is a sound strategy in my opinion as the majority of their industrial knowledge and ability to extract excess revenues lies in these operations. Any expansion beyond this could be successful, but would have a greater element of uncertainty.

Overall, 2024 was a wonderful year for the firm with the 2025 outlook consisting of what management thinks will be a robust demand environment which may be slightly curtailed by labour shortages. My personal expectations are for revenues to grow about 10% YoY organically with acquisitions potentially adding another 8-12% to this figure.

Valuation

One of the best places to start the discussion about what value may lie in FIX shares is the Seeking Alpha Valuation tab. Here, the Quant has provided a “D-“ grade for Comfort Systems stock as compared against the entire industrial sector.

Indeed, the current P/E GAAP TTM of 25x and change is certainly quite elevated although reasonable considering that earnings increased 35% YoY. Similarly, the P/S TTM of 1.87x is quite understandable and suggests that even at current prices, investors would not even be paying twice what the firm’s sales were worth over the TTM.

The P/CF TTM of 15x is quite high although once again, relatively reasonable for a firm growing as fast as Comfort Systems USA. I think the more pessimistic letter grade is due to the cheaper valuations seen within the industrials sector as a result of the slower growth most of these companies experience.

Moving over to the Seeking Alpha Advanced Chart for FIX, we can see the massive performance of shares over the last five-years. When compared to the popular S&P500 tracking SPY index fund (SPY), FIX stock has generated almost 700% excess growth as a result of rapidly growing revenues and earnings boosting their share price.

I also completed a DCF model for Comfort Systems in order to provide an additional quantitatively derived perspective on the value of shares.

FCF and balance sheet data came from the Financials tab for Comfort Systems on Seeking Alpha. My average annualised growth rate of FIX was 41% over the last decade which is truly quite remarkable.

However, I doubt such a massive rate of growth could continue indefinitely which is why is adjusted it down to 8% for the 10Y forecast.

A slower economy in the U.S. combined with some cyclicality would equally reduce the rate at which Comfort Systems could acquire new customers and grow their organic operations. Acquisitions may help grow earnings, but unless the firm can purchase these rivals at discounts the direct return to investors would be limited.

Nevertheless, using this projected growth rate of 8%, a terminal rate of 3% (long-term estimate for inflation and GDP growth) along with a 7.98% discount rate (reflecting the opportunity cost I face for not investing in the S&P500 which produced this return over the last 93-years), I calculated a $670 IV per share.

This would imply shares are 42% undervalued relative to the present value of all future free cashflows to be produced by Comfort Systems USA. While even the 8% growth rate is certainly making some assumptions about their future successes, I don’t think my estimate are particularly risk averse and consider the entire model to err on the side of conservatism if anything.

Of course, the short-term (1-12 months) may be quite volatile as the higher valuation merited by their growth prospects also tends to mean that underperformance could lead to downward pressure.

However, the long-term (1-10 years) outlook for Comfort Systems USA certainly appears positive with great economic earnings power, a strong moat and astute management suggesting that the company is in a prime position to produce outsized returns from operations.

Comfort Systems USA Risk Profile

The firm faces three main risks: market cyclicality, increasing competition and failed execution of acquisitions.

While the firm certainly is going to be susceptible (as was seen in 2016, 2019 and 2021) to the impact slower periods of economic growth will have on demand for new MEP products, I suspect the company may face additional end-market risk from the concentration of their customers in the technology and manufacturing sectors.

Approximately 30% of revenues come from technology end markets and 20% from manufacturing with chip fabricators, data centres and manufacturing plants being the primary customers in this grouping. These highly cyclical sectors could significantly reduce demand for new solutions should there be an excess of capacity in their industries which in turn would harm the overall level of demand for Comfort Systems USA's products.

As a result, and despite the ongoing maintenance revenues Comfort Systems would earn, I suspect quite a large degree of cyclicality in revenues is likely.

Comfort Systems may also see an increase in competition within the MEP industry as their excess returns on invested capital draw-in additional competition. Even without new entrants, rivals like IES certainly have the potential to claw at Comfort Systems’ market share should the firm make any missteps with product development.

The risk of poorly executed acquisitions also could be a real risk facing Comfort Systems. Any overly expensive or non-core purchases of companies could erode both their fiscal underpinnings as well as their earnings power which collectively would harm their economic profile.

In lieu of these risks and what I anticipate will be a large degree of cyclicality, I consider FIX shares to have a high degree of risk and urge all investors to conduct their own research into these matters before making any investment decisions.

Summary

After analysing Comfort Systems, I think I may have just found another wonderful industrial mid-cap business. A wide and robust economic moat, great economic earnings power, a sound balance sheet and what seems to be a solid management team supports my hypothesis that Comfort Systems may be able to produce outsized returns for shareholders over the next 10 years.

Fiscal year 2024 was robust for the firm and while growth did slow compared to years prior, the company remains very profitable and continues to pursue a business strategy revolving around organic growth and acquisitions.

The valuation also appears to present a GARP opportunity with a potentially massive 42% undervaluation currently existing in shares. However, risks certainly exist that could undermine this growth. DCF calculations are also very sensitive to changes in growth rate inputs and I therefore consider this DCF IV result relatively against other valuation data.

Nevertheless, I really like the company, I like the valuation and I like their prospects which is why I award FIX shares a Buy rating at present time.
